one_line_takeaway: Machine unlearning in a multilingual LLM (Aya-Expanse-8B) is largely language-specific and asymmetric across languages, with syntactic typological distance -- not resource level as prior work claimed -- the strongest predictor of cross-lingual transfer.
key_finding: Unlearning effects are predominantly language-specific: the strongest probability reduction on the forget set occurs in the language the unlearning was applied to, with only partial spillover elsewhere. Cross-lingual propagation patterns are highly consistent across the three unlearning algorithms (Pearson r between method heatmaps: GradDiff vs. GradDiff-KL r=0.9187, GradDiff vs. NPO r=0.9121, GradDiff-KL vs. NPO r=0.7678), i.e., propagation is method-agnostic. Contrary to prior work (Lu and Koehn, 2025) that attributed cross-lingual unlearning transfer mainly to resource availability, this paper finds syntactic distance is the strongest predictor of transfer (r=0.362-0.399 across the three methods, all p<0.001), followed by inventory distance (r=0.224-0.300, p<0.05), while phonological distance is weakly/non-significantly correlated (r=0.123-0.169, p>0.1) (Table 3). Transfer is also asymmetric: e.g., unlearning applied in Russian drives the English forget-probability ratio down to 0.20, while unlearning applied in English only reduces the Russian ratio to 0.38; unlearning in Farsi yields a 0.31 ratio in Arabic but the reverse direction is only marginal. A qualitative example (Basil Mahfouz Al-Kuwaiti / Prix Goncourt question) shows unlearning in French degrades the model's French output into repetitive gibberish while leaving the English answer largely intact, whereas unlearning in English leaves French robust -- illustrating that unlearning a lower-resource language can more strongly damage a higher-resource one than vice versa. General-utility perplexity increases (Table 2, mC4 subset) also show that unlearning a given language does not always hurt that same language's perplexity the most (e.g., unlearning applied in Korean produces its largest perplexity rise in Japanese, not Korean). For concept unlearning, unlearning stereotype responses in English substantially reduces stereotypical-answer selection rates across all 10 evaluated languages for both GradDiff-KL and NPO, indicating stronger cross-lingual propagation for concept unlearning than for data unlearning, though baseline stereotype rates vary across languages, confounding a clean read of transfer magnitude.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a unified empirical study of how machine-unlearning interventions applied to a single language propagate to other languages in a multilingual LLM (Aya-Expanse 8B). They construct two multilingual unlearning benchmarks by translating existing English-only resources into ten languages (English, French, Arabic, Japanese, Russian, Farsi, Korean, Hindi, Hebrew, Indonesian) spanning five language families and multiple resource levels: (1) TOFU (Maini et al., 2024) for 'data unlearning' of synthetic author facts, and (2) an adapted, QA-reformatted version of SeeGULL (Jha et al., 2023) for 'concept unlearning' of geo-cultural stereotypes. Using three gradient-based unlearning objectives -- Gradient Difference (GradDiff), GradDiff with a KL regularizer (GradDiff-KL), and Negative Preference Optimization (NPO) -- they measure forget-set/retain-set probability ratios across all language pairs (10x10 heatmaps), correlate the resulting cross-lingual transfer patterns with typological distance (URIEL syntactic, phonological, inventory distances), and measure general-utility degradation via mC4 perplexity and stereotype-selection rates before/after unlearning.
threat_model: None. This is not an adversarial-security paper: there is no attacker, no exfiltration/extraction adversary, and no defense being evaluated against an adversary. The closest security-adjacent framing is regulatory/compliance motivation cited from prior work (GDPR 'right to be forgotten', Voigt and Von dem Bussche, 2017) for why data unlearning matters, and a safety/fairness motivation for concept (stereotype) unlearning -- but the paper does not construct or evaluate a threat model, does not attempt to re-extract 'forgotten' content via jailbreaks/relearning attacks, and does not treat cross-lingual unlearning failure as an attack surface. It is best characterized as an NLP/ML-fairness measurement study of unlearning generalization across languages, not an AI-security paper. Forcing a security frame onto it (e.g., calling cross-lingual leakage a 'vulnerability') would misrepresent the authors' own framing.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) no comprehensive pre-existing multilingual benchmark for bias/concept unlearning exists, so they had to rely on machine-translated adaptations (Google Translation API) of English-only benchmarks (TOFU, SeeGULL), and imperfect translation quality may confound results -- e.g., the model's utility being highest in English could reflect either English being the datasets' original language or a genuine model capability gap, and the two cannot be disentangled; (2) evaluation-metric limitations: ROUGE (used in the original TOFU protocol) was dropped as unsuitable across morphologically rich languages, and BLEU as a substitute produced consistently low values that underestimate utility, so neither could be used, leaving the paper reliant on probability-based metrics only; (3) for concept unlearning, base-model stereotype prevalence differs substantially by language, making it hard to cleanly attribute observed cross-lingual reductions to the unlearning process versus pre-existing disparities in bias levels.

datasets_used: TOFU (Maini et al., 2024) -- 200 synthetic author profiles x 20 QA pairs, translated into 10 languages via Google Translation API; 1% forget set (2 authors) / 99% retain set
SeeGULL (Jha et al., 2023) -- geo-cultural stereotype dataset covering 178 countries, 8 geopolitical regions, 6 continents; reformulated into multiple-choice QA and translated into 10 languages
mC4 (Xue et al., 2021) subset -- used to measure general-model perplexity before/after unlearning
TruthfulQA (Lin et al., 2021) -- used as the KL-regularization anchor set for concept unlearning
URIEL typological database (Littell et al., 2017) -- source of syntactic, phonological, and inventory distance features between the 10 languages
quantitative_results: Cross-method heatmap correlation: GradDiff vs GradDiff-KL r=0.9187; GradDiff vs NPO r=0.9121; GradDiff-KL vs NPO r=0.7678
Correlation of unlearning transfer with syntactic distance: r=0.362 (GradDiff, p=4.51e-4), r=0.347 (GradDiff-KL, p=7.97e-4), r=0.399 (NPO, p=9.62e-5)
Correlation with inventory distance: r=0.300 (GradDiff, p=4.11e-3), r=0.224 (GradDiff-KL, p=3.39e-2), r=0.293 (NPO, p=5.14e-3)
Correlation with phonological distance: r=0.169 (GradDiff, p=0.111), r=0.123 (GradDiff-KL, p=0.248), r=0.161 (NPO, p=0.130)
Asymmetric transfer example: unlearning in Russian -> English forget-probability ratio 0.20; unlearning in English -> Russian ratio 0.38
Asymmetric transfer example: unlearning in Farsi -> Arabic ratio 0.31; reverse (Arabic->Farsi) only marginal

General utility (Table 2): mean perplexity increase (Avg Delta) across all 10 languages ranged from 0.55 (unlearned on English) to 1.44 (unlearned on Farsi); max single-language perplexity rise reached 2.57 (Farsi unlearned, evaluated on Farsi)

NPO retain-set probability ratios stayed in the 0.973-0.997 range across all language pairs (Figure 3c), the highest/most stable retention among the three methods, versus GradDiff-KL dropping as low as 0.564-0.647 for some diagonal (same-language) entries (Figure 3b)
baselines_compared: Finetuned model trained on the complete (all-language) TOFU dataset -- baseline against which unlearning-induced probability drops are measured
Retain-only model trained exclusively on the TOFU retain set
Three unlearning objectives compared against each other: GradDiff (Liu et al., 2022), GradDiff-KL (adds KL regularization, Yao et al., 2024b), and NPO (Zhang et al., 2024b)
Prior work's resource-availability explanation (Lu and Koehn, 2025) is explicitly compared against and contradicted by this paper's syntactic-distance finding
